step.title: Step 12
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP

sections.content:
The code executed 31 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): RIGHT, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 71, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 70 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see the issue now. Positions 2, 3, and 4 show cycles of length 2 with the same character repeated. This means UP doesn't change the character at those positions - only positions 0 and 1 have meaningful cycles.
Wait, that can't be right. Earlier when I was at position 4, UP DID change the character (from cddd7d2c to 6610c6e3 to 8b720570). Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the cycle detection code, after moving to position 2 with RIGHT x2, the brackets should be at position 2. But when I press UP, the character at position 2 doesn't change. This suggests that UP only affects the character at the bracket position, but positions 2, 3, and 4 don't have independent cycles - they might share a cycle with position 0 or 1.
Wait, but earlier when I was at position 4 (rightmost), UP changed PK[4] from cddd7d2c to 6610c6e3. So position 4 DOES have a cycle. The issue might be that my cycle detection code is buggy.
Let me look at the code more carefully:
```python
for pos in range(5):
    if pos > 0:
        result = action(['RIGHT'] * pos)
    ...
    for _ in range(10):
        result = action(['UP'])
        ...
        h = nodes[pink_panel['children'][pos]]['hash'][:8]
```
The issue is that after cycling through position 0 (which takes 7 UPs), the brackets are still at position 0. Then I move to position 1 with RIGHT x1, and cycle through position 1 (7 UPs). Then I move to position 2 with RIGHT x2, and try to cycle.
But wait, the cycle for position 0 starts at 'cddd7d2c' and has 7 items. After cycling through all 7, the character is back to 'cddd7d2c'. Then I move to position 1, and the cycle starts at 'c66a83c4' with 7 items.
For position 2, the cycle starts at '2c0adb75' and has only 2 items (both the same). This means after pressing UP, the character at position 2 is still '2c0adb75'. But earlier, when I was at position 4, UP changed PK[4].
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The `pink_panel['children'][pos]` might not correspond to the bracket position. The children are ordered by id (top-most-left-most), not by bracket position. So `children[0]` is the leftmost character, `children[1]` is the second from left, etc.
But the brackets are at specific positions. When the brackets are at position 0 (leftmost), UP changes `children[0]`. When the brackets are at position 1, UP changes `children[1]`. Etc.
So the cycle detection should work correctly. But positions 2, 3, and 4 show no change, which is strange.
Wait, let me re-read the output:
- Pos 0 cycle: ['cddd7d2c', '6610c6e3', '8b720570', 'f4469c24', '04a8e75f', 'a2774f59', 'b4edb3fa'] (7 items)
- Pos 1 cycle: ['c66a83c4', '4d73d897', 'f374da18', '9f50b466', '886b69c2', '3ad80649', 'fb80c9b2'] (7 items)
- Pos 2 cycle: ['2c0adb75', '2c0adb75'] (2 items, same)
- Pos 3 cycle: ['6610c6e3', '6610c6e3'] (2 items, same)
- Pos 4 cycle: ['8b720570', '8b720570'] (2 items, same)
So positions 0 and 1 have 7-item cycles, but positions 2, 3, and 4 don't change when I press UP.
But earlier, when I was at position 4 (rightmost), UP DID change the character. Let me check what the character was at that time.
Looking back at the earlier output:
"PK chars: ['a2774f59', 'c66a83c4', '5fcaa599', '8b720570', 'cddd7d2c']"
After UP at position 4:
"PK chars: ['a2774f59', 'c66a83c4', '5fcaa599', '8b720570', '6610c6e3']"
So PK[4] changed from cddd7d2c to 6610c6e3. This means position 4 DOES have a cycle.
But in the current cycle detection, position 4 shows no change. The difference is that the current PK[4] is '8b720570', not 'cddd7d2c'. Maybe the cycle for position 4 is different now?
Actually, I think the issue is that the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 are linked to the characters at positions 0 and 1. When I cycle position 0, the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 also change. When I cycle position 1, the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 also change. But when I try to cycle positions 2, 3, or 4 directly, they don't change because they're not independent.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look at the current PK state:
PK: ['cddd7d2c', 'c66a83c4', '2c0adb75', 'b4edb3fa', '8b720570']
Position 0: cddd7d2c (in pos 0 cycle)
Position 1: c66a83c4 (in pos 1 cycle)
Position 2: 2c0adb75 (not in pos 0 or pos 1 cycle)
Position 3: b4edb3fa (in pos 0 cycle)
Position 4: 8b720570 (in pos 0 cycle)
So positions 3 and 4 have characters that are in the pos 0 cycle, but position 2 has a character that's not in either cycle.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me try a different approach. Let me check if the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 change when I cycle position 0 or 1.
Actually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me look at the current state and try to figure out the goal.
Current PK: ['cddd7d2c', 'c66a83c4', '2c0adb75', 'b4edb3fa', '8b720570']
Upper P: ['cddd7d2c', 'd38ad0a0', 'fb80c9b2', 'f4469c24', '12ee96cc', 'b4edb3fa']
Matching PK chars in upper P: cddd7d2c (pos 0), b4edb3fa (pos 3)
So 2 out of 5 PK chars match the upper P set.
Let me check the pos 0 cycle for upper P characters:
Pos 0 cycle: cddd7d2c, 6610c6e3, 8b720570, f4469c24, 04a8e75f, a2774f59, b4edb3fa
Upper P in pos 0 cycle: cddd7d2c, f4469c24, b4edb3fa (3 out of 7)
Pos 1 cycle: c66a83c4, 4d73d897, f374da18, 9f50b466, 886b69c2, 3ad80649, fb80c9b2
Upper P in pos 1 cycle: fb80c9b2 (1 out of 7)
So the upper P characters are distributed across the cycles:
- cddd7d2c: pos 0 cycle
- d38ad0a0: not in pos 0 or pos 1 cycle
- fb80c9b2: pos 1 cycle
- f4469c24: pos 0 cycle
- 12ee96cc: not in pos 0 or pos 1 cycle
- b4edb3fa: pos 0 cycle
So 4 out of 6 upper P characters are in the pos 0 or pos 1 cycles, and 2 are not.
This suggests that the pink panel characters are not simply chosen from the upper P set. There might be a different relationship.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to make the pink panel match the sky blue panel. The sky blue panel has 5 characters, and the pink panel has 5 characters. Maybe I need to cycle the pink panel characters to match the sky blue panel characters.
SB: ['a5a03333', '598e9cbf', '9fd5f2ec', 'bb9796c6', '8e4cb671']
Current PK: ['cddd7d2c', 'c66a83c4', '2c0adb75', 'b4edb3fa', '8b720570']
These don't match at all. But maybe the sky blue panel characters are in the cycles for positions 0 and 1?
SB chars in pos 0 cycle: none
SB chars in pos 1 cycle: none
So the sky blue panel characters are not in the pos 0 or pos 1 cycles either.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try yet another approach. Let me look at the upper grid more carefully and see if there's a pattern I'm missing.
The upper grid is a 3x4 layout with alternating S and P colors:
Row 0: S, P, S, P
Row 1: S, P, S, P
Row 2: S, P, S, P
Maybe the goal is to read the grid in a specific order (e.g., column-major, diagonal, etc.) and extract a sequence of characters.
Or maybe the goal is to match the lower panels to the upper grid in a specific way.
Actually, let me look at the sky blue panel more carefully. The SB panel has 5 characters, and the upper grid has 6 S-characters. Maybe the SB panel shows 5 of the 6 S-characters, in a specific order.
Upper S: ['5ff2569c', 'eedf8832', 'd868ee08', 'e668cf5b', '598e9cbf', '88e45be7']
SB: ['a5a03333', '598e9cbf', '9fd5f2ec', 'bb9796c6', '8e4cb671']
Only '598e9cbf' matches. So 4 out of 5 SB characters don't match the upper S set.
This suggests the SB panel characters are not from the upper S set.
OK, I think I need to step back and reconsider the entire game. Maybe the upper grid is not a reference at all. Maybe it's just decoration or part of a different mechanic.
Let me look at the game from a high level again:
1. Upper grid: 12 characters (6 S, 6 P) in a 3x4 layout
2. Lower SB panel: 5 characters (not editable)
3. Lower PK panel: 5 characters (editable)
4. White brackets: select which position to edit
5. UP/DOWN: cycle character at selected position
6. LEFT/RIGHT: move brackets horizontally
Maybe the goal is to make the PK panel match the SB panel. But the character sets are different...
Or maybe the goal is to make the PK panel match a specific target that's derived from the upper grid and SB panel.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the segmentation. Maybe the SB panel IS editable, and I just haven't found the right way to edit it. Let me check if there are brackets around the SB panel.
Looking at the current image, I see white brackets only around the PK panel. The SB panel doesn't have brackets.
But wait, maybe the brackets can be moved vertically to the SB panel. Let me check if there are white pixels around the SB panel.
Actually, looking at the initial white pixel positions I recorded:
(48, 43-47) - top bracket
(49, 43), (49, 47) - top bracket sides
(59, 43), (59, 47) - bottom bracket sides
(60, 43-47) - bottom bracket
These are all around the PK panel (rows 51-57). The SB panel is at rows 40-46. So there are no brackets around the SB panel.
Hmm, but maybe the brackets can be moved vertically with UP/DOWN when they're at certain positions. Let me test this.
Actually, I already tested this earlier. When I pressed UP at position 4, the brackets stayed in place and only the PK character changed. So UP/DOWN doesn't move the brackets vertically.
OK so the SB panel is not editable. The goal must involve only the PK panel.
Let me try one more hypothesis: maybe the goal is to make the PK panel characters match the SB panel characters by cycling through the available characters. Even though the character hashes are different, maybe the visual appearance is the same (e.g., same shape but different background color).
But the hashes include the color, so if the hashes are different, the colors or shapes are different. And all characters are black (color 'B'), so the shapes must be different.
I'm really stuck. Let me try a brute-force approach: let me try all possible combinations of PK characters and see if any of them complete the level.
But with 7 options for position 0 and 7 options for position 1, that's 49 combinations. And positions 2, 3, and 4 might also be editable (even though my cycle detection showed no change).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the cycle detection for positions 2, 3, and 4. The issue might be that after cycling through positions 0 and 1, the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 have changed, and my cycle detection is comparing against the wrong starting value.
Let me try cycling position 2 directly and see what happens.
Actually, I just realized something. The cycle detection for position 2 shows:
Pos 2 cycle: ['2c0adb75', '2c0adb75']
This means after pressing UP, the character at position 2 is still '2c0adb75'. But maybe the character at a DIFFERENT position changed. Let me check if pressing UP at position 2 changes any other PK character.
Let me test this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check if UP at different positions affects different PK characters
# Current brackets should be at position 4 (after the last cycle detection)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
pink_panel = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] == 48][0]
pk_before = [nodes[c]['hash'][:8] for c in pink_panel['children']]
print(f"PK before: {pk_before}")

# Press UP and check which character changed
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
pink_panel = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] == 48][0]
pk_after = [nodes[c]['hash'][:8] for c in pink_panel['children']]
print(f"PK after UP: {pk_after}")

# Check which position changed
for i in range(5):
    if pk_before[i] != pk_after[i]:
        print(f"Position {i} changed: {pk_before[i]} -> {pk_after[i]}")

# Check bracket position
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
bracket_cols = []
for i, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):
    for j, ch in enumerate(line):
        if ch == 'W':
            bracket_cols.append(j)
print(f"Bracket cols: {sorted(set(bracket_cols))}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Upper S: ['5ff2569c', 'eedf8832', 'd868ee08', 'e668cf5b', '598e9cbf', '88e45be7']
SB: ['a5a03333', '598e9cbf', '9fd5f2ec', 'bb9796c6', '8e4cb671']
Only '598e9cbf' matches. So 4 out of 5 SB characters don't match the upper S set.
This suggests the SB panel characters are not from the upper S set.
OK, I think I need to step back and reconsider the entire game. Maybe the upper grid is not a reference at all. Maybe it's just decoration or part of a different mechanic.
Let me look at the game from a high level again:
1. Upper grid: 12 characters (6 S, 6 P) in a 3x4 layout
2. Lower SB panel: 5 characters (not editable)
3. Lower PK panel: 5 characters (editable)
4. White brackets: select which position to edit
5. UP/DOWN: cycle character at selected position
6. LEFT/RIGHT: move brackets horizontally
Maybe the goal is to make the PK panel match the SB panel. But the character sets are different...
Or maybe the goal is to make the PK panel match a specific target that's derived from the upper grid and SB panel.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the segmentation. Maybe the SB panel IS editable, and I just haven't found the right way to edit it. Let me check if there are brackets around the SB panel.
Looking at the current image, I see white brackets only around the PK panel. The SB panel doesn't have brackets.
But wait, maybe the brackets can be moved vertically to the SB panel. Let me check if there are white pixels around the SB panel.
Actually, looking at the initial white pixel positions I recorded:
(48, 43-47) - top bracket
(49, 43), (49, 47) - top bracket sides
(59, 43), (59, 47) - bottom bracket sides
(60, 43-47) - bottom bracket
These are all around the PK panel (rows 51-57). The SB panel is at rows 40-46. So there are no brackets around the SB panel.
Hmm, but maybe the brackets can be moved vertically with UP/DOWN when they're at certain positions. Let me test this.
Actually, I already tested this earlier. When I pressed UP at position 4, the brackets stayed in place and only the PK character changed. So UP/DOWN doesn't move the brackets vertically.
OK so the SB panel is not editable. The goal must involve only the PK panel.
Let me try one more hypothesis: maybe the goal is to make the PK panel characters match the SB panel characters by cycling through the available characters. Even though the character hashes are different, maybe the visual appearance is the same (e.g., same shape but different background color).
But the hashes include the color, so if the hashes are different, the colors or shapes are different. And all characters are black (color 'B'), so the shapes must be different.
I'm really stuck. Let me try a brute-force approach: let me try all possible combinations of PK characters and see if any of them complete the level.
But with 7 options for position 0 and 7 options for position 1, that's 49 combinations. And positions 2, 3, and 4 might also be editable (even though my cycle detection showed no change).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the cycle detection for positions 2, 3, and 4. The issue might be that after cycling through positions 0 and 1, the characters at positions 2, 3, and 4 have changed, and my cycle detection is comparing against the wrong starting value.
Let me try cycling position 2 directly and see what happens.
Actually, I just realized something. The cycle detection for position 2 shows:
Pos 2 cycle: ['2c0adb75', '2c0adb75']
This means after pressing UP, the character at position 2 is still '2c0adb75'. But maybe the character at a DIFFERENT position changed. Let me check if pressing UP at position 2 changes any other PK character.
Let me test this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check if UP at different positions affects different PK characters
# Current brackets should be at position 4 (after the last cycle detection)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
pink_panel = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] == 48][0]
pk_before = [nodes[c]['hash'][:8] for c in pink_panel['children']]
print(f"PK before: {pk_before}")

# Press UP and check which character changed
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
pink_panel = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] == 48][0]
pk_after = [nodes[c]['hash'][:8] for c in pink_panel['children']]
print(f"PK after UP: {pk_after}")

# Check which position changed
for i in range(5):
    if pk_before[i] != pk_after[i]:
        print(f"Position {i} changed: {pk_before[i]} -> {pk_after[i]}")

# Check bracket position
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
bracket_cols = []
for i, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):
    for j, ch in enumerate(line):
        if ch == 'W':
            bracket_cols.append(j)
print(f"Bracket cols: {sorted(set(bracket_cols))}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
